Part One (Main Methods) is organized wit.reason: The article clearly organizes Part One into three categories (exploration-based, model-based, optimization-based), each with principles, key algorithms, and advantages/disadvantages, and does not cite any blocked reference.
Part One (Main Methods) is organized wit.evidence: Part One: Main Methods. (1) Exploration-based methods: These methods frame inverse design as an optimization or search over a large compositional/structural space. Key algorithms include genetic algorithms (GA), which evolve a population of candidates through selection, crossover, and mutation; Bayesian optimization (BO), which builds a probabilistic surrogate model (often a Gaussian process) to guide sampling toward promising regions; and reinforcement learning (RL), where an agent learns a policy to sequentially propose new materials. Advantages: GA is simple and effective for discrete spaces; BO is sample-efficient for expensive evaluations; RL can handle sequential decision-making. Disadvantages: GA can be slow for high-dimensional spaces; BO may struggle with highly multimodal landscapes; RL requires careful reward design. (2) Model-based methods: These methods learn a generative model that directly produces material candidates conditioned on desired properties. Key algorithms include generative adversarial networks (GANs), which consist of a generator and discriminator trained adversarially; variational autoencoders (VAEs), which learn a latent representation and generate new samples by decoding points in latent space; and normalizing flows, which learn an invertible mapping between a simple base distribution and the data distribution. Advantages: GANs can generate high-quality samples; VAEs provide a structured latent space; normalizing flows offer exact likelihood estimation. Disadvantages: GANs suffer from mode collapse; VAEs may produce blurry samples; normalizing flows are computationally expensive. (3) Optimization-based methods: These methods leverage differentiable models to iteratively refine material representations by backpropagating property gradients. Key algorithms include gradient-based optimization (e.g., using neural network surrogates) and adjoint methods for topology optimization. Advantages: Direct gradient information enables efficient local refinement; well-suited for continuous design spaces. Disadvantages: Require differentiable representations of materials; many property calculations (e.g., DFT) are non-differentiable, limiting applicability.

Part Two (Key Databases) is presented in.reason: The article presents Part Two as a list of databases, each with name, description, and URL, and does not cite any blocked reference.
Part Two (Key Databases) is presented in.evidence: Part Two: Key Databases. (1) Materials Project (materialsproject.org): Provides computed properties (band gaps, formation energies, etc.) for over 150,000 inorganic compounds. (2) OQMD (oqmd.org): Contains ~1 million DFT-calculated entries for a wide range of materials. (3) AFLOW (aflowlib.org): Offers automated crystal structure prediction and a large repository of computed properties with standardized descriptors. (4) NOMAD (nomad-lab.eu): Focuses on raw and processed computational data from many sources, supporting FAIR data principles. (5) Citrination (citrination.com): A platform for materials data management and ML model deployment, with both public and private datasets.
